verses.text_hebrew: וַיְהִ֖י אַחֲרֵ֣י הַמַּגֵּפָ֑ה {פ}וַיֹּ֤אמֶר יְהֹוָה֙ אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֔ה וְאֶ֧ל אֶלְעָזָ֛ר בֶּן־אַהֲרֹ֥ן הַכֹּהֵ֖ן לֵאמֹֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: And it came to pass after the plague, that the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Eleazar the son of Aaron the priest, saying,
verses.rendering: After the plague, the LORD spoke to Moses and to Eleazar son of Aaron the priest, saying:
translator_notes: The hammageifah ('the plague') refers to the devastating epidemic at Baal Peor (Numbers 25:9) that killed 24,000. This second census takes place in the aftermath — the people must be recounted because the first generation has perished. Notably, Eleazar replaces his father Aaron (who died in Numbers 20:28) as the priestly partner in the census, marking the generational transition.

key_terms.note: From nagaf ('to strike, smite'); a divinely-sent epidemic as judgment for the Baal Peor apostasy

verses.text_hebrew: שְׂא֞וּ אֶת־רֹ֣אשׁ ׀ כׇּל־עֲדַ֣ת בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל מִבֶּ֨ן עֶשְׂרִ֥ים שָׁנָ֛ה וָמַ֖עְלָה לְבֵ֣ית אֲבֹתָ֑ם כׇּל־יֹצֵ֥א צָבָ֖א בְּיִשְׂרָאֵֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: Take the sum of all the congregation of the children of Israel, from twenty years old and upward, throughout their fathers' house, all that are able to go to war in Israel.
verses.rendering: Count the entire Israelite community by their ancestral households — every male from twenty years old and above who is eligible for military service in Israel.
translator_notes: The idiom se'u et-rosh ('lift the head') means 'take a count/census.' The census parameters match Numbers 1:2-3 exactly: mibben esrim shanah vama'lah ('from twenty years and above'), leveit avotam ('by their ancestral households'), and kol-yotze tzava ('all going out to the army'). This deliberate mirroring invites comparison between the two generations.

key_terms.semantic_range: lift the head, take a census, count, number
key_terms.note: Literally 'lift the head of'; a dignifying idiom for census-taking that treats each person as individually significant

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְדַבֵּ֨ר מֹשֶׁ֜ה וְאֶלְעָזָ֧ר הַכֹּהֵ֛ן אֹתָ֖ם בְּעַֽרְבֹ֣ת מוֹאָ֑ב עַל־יַרְדֵּ֥ן יְרֵח֖וֹ לֵאמֹֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: And Moses and Eleazar the priest spake with them in the plains of Moab by Jordan near Jericho, saying,
verses.rendering: Moses and Eleazar the priest addressed them in the plains of Moab, beside the Jordan across from Jericho:
translator_notes: The location be'arvot Mo'av ('in the plains/steppes of Moab') al-Yarden Yerecho ('by the Jordan at Jericho') places this census at the doorstep of the Promised Land — geographically opposite to the first census at Sinai. The name arvot ('plains, arid steppes') describes the flat terrain east of the Jordan near the Dead Sea.

verses.text_hebrew: מִבֶּ֛ן עֶשְׂרִ֥ים שָׁנָ֖ה וָמָ֑עְלָה כַּאֲשֶׁר֩ צִוָּ֨ה יְהֹוָ֤ה אֶת־מֹשֶׁה֙ וּבְנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל הַיֹּצְאִ֖ים מֵאֶ֥רֶץ מִצְרָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: Take the sum of the people, from twenty years old and upward; as the LORD commanded Moses and the children of Israel, which went forth out of the land of Egypt.
verses.rendering: From twenty years old and above, just as the LORD had commanded Moses. These are the Israelites who came out of the land of Egypt:
translator_notes: The phrase ka'asher tzivvah YHVH et-Mosheh ('as the LORD commanded Moses') grounds the census in divine authority. The closing reference uvenei Yisra'el hayyotze'im me'eretz Mitzrayim ('and the Israelites who went out from the land of Egypt') transitions from instructions to the actual census list, beginning with the exodus generation's descendants.

verses.text_hebrew: רְאוּבֵ֖ן בְּכ֣וֹר יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל בְּנֵ֣י רְאוּבֵ֗ן חֲנוֹךְ֙ מִשְׁפַּ֣חַת הַחֲנֹכִ֔י לְפַלּ֕וּא מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַפַּלֻּאִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Reuben, the eldest son of Israel: the children of Reuben; Hanoch, of whom cometh the family of the Hanochites: of Pallu, the family of the Palluites:
verses.rendering: Reuben, Israel's firstborn. The descendants of Reuben: from Hanoch, the Hanochite clan; from Pallu, the Palluite clan.
translator_notes: The census begins with Re'uven bekhor Yisra'el ('Reuben, firstborn of Israel'), honoring the birth order despite Reuben's loss of the firstborn privileges (Genesis 49:3-4). Each son generates a mishpachah ('clan/family') with a gentilic adjective — HaChanokhi ('the Hanochite'). The clan structure tracks tribal subdivisions for military organization and land allocation.

key_terms.semantic_range: clan, family, extended family group, sub-tribe
key_terms.note: The intermediate social unit between the tribe (matteh/shevet) and the household (beit av)

verses.text_hebrew: לְחֶצְרֹ֕ן מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַֽחֶצְרוֹנִ֑י לְכַרְמִ֕י מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַכַּרְמִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Of Hezron, the family of the Hezronites: of Carmi, the family of the Carmites.
verses.rendering: From Hezron, the Hezronite clan; from Carmi, the Carmite clan.
translator_notes: Chetzron and Karmi complete Reuben's four clans. These names are consistent with Genesis 46:9 and Exodus 6:14, maintaining genealogical continuity across the Torah. The gentilic forms (HaChetzroni, HaKarmi) use the standard -i suffix to denote clan membership.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֖לֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹ֣ת הָראוּבֵנִ֑י וַיִּהְי֣וּ פְקֻדֵיהֶ֗ם שְׁלֹשָׁ֤ה וְאַרְבָּעִים֙ אֶ֔לֶף וּשְׁבַ֥ע מֵא֖וֹת וּשְׁלֹשִֽׁים׃
verses.text_kjv: These are the families of the Reubenites: and they that were numbered of them were forty and three thousand and seven hundred and thirty.
verses.rendering: These are the Reubenite clans. Their registered total was 43,730.
translator_notes: Reuben's second census total of 43,730 represents a decrease from the first census figure of 46,500 (Numbers 1:21) — a loss of 2,770. The term pequdehem ('their registered ones, their counted ones') derives from paqad ('to attend to, number, muster'). The decline may reflect the toll of wilderness judgments on this tribe.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבְנֵ֥י פַלּ֖וּא אֱלִיאָֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: And the sons of Pallu; Eliab.
verses.rendering: The son of Pallu was Eliab.
translator_notes: The singular benei Pallu ('sons of Pallu') yielding only Eli'av ('my God is father') narrows the genealogy to a single line of descent. This brevity sets up the important digression in verses 9-11 about Dathan, Abiram, and Korah — figures from this very clan.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבְנֵ֣י אֱלִיאָ֔ב נְמוּאֵ֖ל וְדָתָ֣ן וַאֲבִירָ֑ם הֽוּא־דָתָ֨ן וַאֲבִירָ֜ם (קרואי) [קְרִיאֵ֣י] הָעֵדָ֗ה אֲשֶׁ֨ר הִצּ֜וּ עַל־מֹשֶׁ֤ה וְעַֽל־אַהֲרֹן֙ בַּעֲדַת־קֹ֔רַח בְּהַצֹּתָ֖ם עַל־יְהֹוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And the sons of Eliab; Nemuel, and Dathan, and Abiram. This is that Dathan and Abiram, which were famous in the congregation, who strove against Moses and against Aaron in the company of Korah, when they strove against the LORD:
verses.rendering: The sons of Eliab were Nemuel, Dathan, and Abiram. These are the same Dathan and Abiram — chosen representatives of the community — who contended against Moses and Aaron as part of Korah's faction when they challenged the LORD.
translator_notes: The parenthetical hu-Datan va'Aviram ('this is the same Dathan and Abiram') inserts a historical cross-reference into the census list. They were qeri'ei ha'edah ('the ones called/summoned by the community') — prominent leaders, not obscure figures. The ketiv-qere variant (qrw'y / qeri'ei) reflects textual transmission history. Their rebellion ba'adat-Qorach ('in the faction of Korah') is narrated in Numbers 16.

verses.text_hebrew: וַתִּפְתַּ֨ח הָאָ֜רֶץ אֶת־פִּ֗יהָ וַתִּבְלַ֥ע אֹתָ֛ם וְאֶת־קֹ֖רַח בְּמ֣וֹת הָעֵדָ֑ה בַּאֲכֹ֣ל הָאֵ֗שׁ אֵ֣ת חֲמִשִּׁ֤ים וּמָאתַ֙יִם֙ אִ֔ישׁ וַיִּהְי֖וּ לְנֵֽס׃
verses.text_kjv: And the earth opened her mouth, and swallowed them up together with Korah, when that company died, what time the fire devoured two hundred and fifty men: and they became a sign.
verses.rendering: The earth opened its mouth and swallowed them along with Korah, when that faction died — when the fire consumed two hundred and fifty men. They became a warning sign.
translator_notes: The vivid vatiftach ha'aretz et-piha ('the earth opened its mouth') personifies the ground as an agent of divine judgment. The phrase vayyihyu lanes ('they became a sign/warning') indicates their fate served as an enduring cautionary example. The word nes here means 'warning sign' or 'signal' rather than its later sense of 'miracle.' The death of the 250 incense-burners by fire (ba'akhol ha'esh) is distinct from the ground's swallowing of Korah.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבְנֵי־קֹ֖רַח לֹא־מֵֽתוּ׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: Notwithstanding the children of Korah died not.
verses.rendering: But Korah's descendants did not die.
translator_notes: This brief but theologically significant note — uvenei-Qorach lo-metu ('the sons of Korah did not die') — explains how Korahite clans survived to become Temple singers and psalm composers (see Psalm titles: 42, 44-49, 84-85, 87-88). God's judgment targeted the rebels, not their innocent offspring, demonstrating that guilt is not automatically inherited.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּנֵ֣י שִׁמְעוֹן֮ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם֒ לִנְמוּאֵ֗ל מִשְׁפַּ֙חַת֙ הַנְּמ֣וּאֵלִ֔י לְיָמִ֕ין מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַיָּמִינִ֑י לְיָכִ֕ין מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַיָּכִינִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: The sons of Simeon after their families: of Nemuel, the family of the Nemuelites: of Jamin, the family of the Jaminites: of Jachin, the family of the Jachinites:
verses.rendering: The descendants of Simeon by their clans: from Nemuel, the Nemuelite clan; from Jamin, the Jaminite clan; from Jachin, the Jachinite clan.
translator_notes: Simeon's clan list here differs from Genesis 46:10 — Ohad is absent (perhaps absorbed into another clan), and the names show slight variations (Nemuel vs. Jemuel). The lemishpechotam ('by their clans') introduces the standard clan-listing formula that repeats for each tribe.

verses.text_hebrew: לְזֶ֕רַח מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַזַּרְחִ֑י לְשָׁא֕וּל מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַשָּׁאוּלִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Of Zerah, the family of the Zarhites: of Shaul, the family of the Shaulites.
verses.rendering: From Zerah, the Zerahite clan; from Shaul, the Shaulite clan.
translator_notes: Zerach and Sha'ul complete Simeon's five surviving clans (down from six in Genesis 46:10). Sha'ul is identified in Genesis 46:10 as the son of a Canaanite woman, making him unique among the tribal ancestors. The clan name haZarchi uses the standard gentilic suffix.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֖לֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹ֣ת הַשִּׁמְעֹנִ֑י שְׁנַ֧יִם וְעֶשְׂרִ֛ים אֶ֖לֶף וּמָאתָֽיִם׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: These are the families of the Simeonites, twenty and two thousand and two hundred.
verses.rendering: These are the Simeonite clans. Their total was 22,200.
translator_notes: Simeon suffered the most dramatic decline of any tribe: from 59,300 (Numbers 1:23) to 22,200 — a loss of 37,100, nearly 63%. Rabbinic tradition connects this massive reduction to the Baal Peor incident, where the ringleader Zimri was a Simeonite leader (Numbers 25:14). Simeon would later be absorbed into Judah's territory (Joshua 19:1).

verses.text_hebrew: בְּנֵ֣י גָד֮ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם֒ לִצְפ֗וֹן מִשְׁפַּ֙חַת֙ הַצְּפוֹנִ֔י לְחַגִּ֕י מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַֽחַגִּ֑י לְשׁוּנִ֕י מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַשּׁוּנִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: The children of Gad after their families: of Zephon, the family of the Zephonites: of Haggi, the family of the Haggites: of Shuni, the family of the Shunites:
verses.rendering: The descendants of Gad by their clans: from Zephon, the Zephonite clan; from Haggi, the Haggite clan; from Shuni, the Shunite clan.
translator_notes: Gad's clan list begins with Tzefon (called Ziphion in Genesis 46:16), a name possibly meaning 'watchman' or 'north.' Gad had seven clans — a large number reflecting the tribe's military significance (cf. Genesis 49:19, 'a raiding band shall raid him'). The gentilic HaTzefoni follows the standard pattern.

verses.text_hebrew: לְאׇזְנִ֕י מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הָאׇזְנִ֑י לְעֵרִ֕י מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הָעֵרִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Of Ozni, the family of the Oznites: of Eri, the family of the Erites:
verses.rendering: From Ozni, the Oznite clan; from Eri, the Erite clan.
translator_notes: Ozni (called Ezbon in Genesis 46:16) and Eri continue Gad's clan listings. The name Ozni may derive from ozen ('ear'), possibly meaning 'attentive.' These variant name forms between Genesis and Numbers reflect natural development in oral and scribal transmission.

verses.text_hebrew: לַאֲר֕וֹד מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הָאֲרוֹדִ֑י לְאַ֨רְאֵלִ֔י מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הָאַרְאֵלִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Of Arod, the family of the Arodites: of Areli, the family of the Arelites.
verses.rendering: From Arod, the Arodite clan; from Areli, the Arelite clan.
translator_notes: Arod (called Arodi in Genesis 46:16) and Ar'eli complete Gad's seven clans. Ar'eli may mean 'lion of God' (ari + El), though the etymology is uncertain. The slight name variations (Arodi/Arod) between Genesis and Numbers show the flexibility of Hebrew name forms.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֛לֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹ֥ת בְּנֵֽי־גָ֖ד לִפְקֻדֵיהֶ֑ם אַרְבָּעִ֥ים אֶ֖לֶף וַחֲמֵ֥שׁ מֵאֽוֹת׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: These are the families of the children of Gad according to those that were numbered of them, forty thousand and five hundred.
verses.rendering: These are the clans of the Gadites. Their registered total was 40,500.
translator_notes: Gad decreased from 45,650 (Numbers 1:25) to 40,500 — a reduction of 5,150. The term lifqudehem ('according to their registered ones') uses the same counting vocabulary as verse 7. Gad would later settle east of the Jordan in the territory of Gilead (Numbers 32).

verses.text_hebrew: בְּנֵ֥י יְהוּדָ֖ה עֵ֣ר וְאוֹנָ֑ן וַיָּ֥מׇת עֵ֛ר וְאוֹנָ֖ן בְּאֶ֥רֶץ כְּנָֽעַן׃
verses.text_kjv: The sons of Judah were Er and Onan: and Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan.
verses.rendering: The sons of Judah included Er and Onan, but Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan.
translator_notes: The census pauses to note that Er and Onan — Judah's firstborn and second son — vayamot ('died') be'eretz Kena'an ('in the land of Canaan') before the descent to Egypt. Their deaths (Genesis 38:7-10) eliminated two potential clan lines. Judah's surviving clans descend from his remaining sons Shelah, Perez, and Zerah.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּהְי֣וּ בְנֵי־יְהוּדָה֮ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם֒ לְשֵׁלָ֗ה מִשְׁפַּ֙חַת֙ הַשֵּׁ֣לָנִ֔י לְפֶ֕רֶץ מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַפַּרְצִ֑י לְזֶ֕רַח מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַזַּרְחִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: And the sons of Judah after their families were; of Shelah, the family of the Shelanites: of Pharez, the family of the Pharzites: of Zerah, the family of the Zarhites.
verses.rendering: The descendants of Judah by their clans: from Shelah, the Shelanite clan; from Perez, the Perezite clan; from Zerah, the Zerahite clan.
translator_notes: Judah's three surviving sons generate three primary clans. Peretz ('breach') — born when he 'broke through' before his twin Zerah (Genesis 38:29) — becomes the dominant line through which David and the Messianic lineage descend. The clan of Shelah appears first, following birth order among the survivors.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּהְי֣וּ בְנֵי־פֶ֔רֶץ לְחֶצְרֹ֕ן מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַֽחֶצְרֹנִ֑י לְחָמ֕וּל מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הֶחָמוּלִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: And the sons of Pharez were; of Hezron, the family of the Hezronites: of Hamul, the family of the Hamulites.
verses.rendering: The sons of Perez were: from Hezron, the Hezronite clan; from Hamul, the Hamulite clan.
translator_notes: Perez's sub-clans Chetzron and Chamul represent a second level of genealogical depth — clans within a clan. Chetzron's line is especially important: through him come Ram, Amminadab, Nahshon (the tribal prince), and eventually David (Ruth 4:18-22). This extra genealogical layer signals Judah's growing prominence.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֛לֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹ֥ת יְהוּדָ֖ה לִפְקֻדֵיהֶ֑ם שִׁשָּׁ֧ה וְשִׁבְעִ֛ים אֶ֖לֶף וַחֲמֵ֥שׁ מֵאֽוֹת׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: These are the families of Judah according to those that were numbered of them, threescore and sixteen thousand and five hundred.
verses.rendering: These are the clans of Judah. Their registered total was 76,500.
translator_notes: Judah increased from 74,600 (Numbers 1:27) to 76,500 — a gain of 1,900. Judah remains the largest tribe in both censuses, befitting its role as the leading tribe in march order (Numbers 2:3) and Jacob's royal blessing (Genesis 49:8-12). The term 'threescore' in the KJV renders shishah veshiv'im ('six and seventy').

verses.text_hebrew: בְּנֵ֤י יִשָּׂשכָר֙ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָ֔ם תּוֹלָ֕ע מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַתּוֹלָעִ֑י לְפֻוָ֕ה מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַפּוּנִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Of the sons of Issachar after their families: of Tola, the family of the Tolaites: of Pua, the family of the Punites:
verses.rendering: The descendants of Issachar by their clans: from Tola, the Tolaite clan; from Puah, the Punite clan.
translator_notes: Issachar's clans begin with Tola ('worm, crimson') — a name associated with the crimson dye-producing insect. Puvah (here leFuvah, with the gentilic HaPuni) shows a variant spelling from Genesis 46:13's Puvvah. The four Issachar clans remain stable between the two census lists.

verses.text_hebrew: לְיָשׁ֕וּב מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַיָּשֻׁבִ֑י לְשִׁמְרֹ֕ן מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַשִּׁמְרֹנִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Of Jashub, the family of the Jashubites: of Shimron, the family of the Shimronites.
verses.rendering: From Jashub, the Jashubite clan; from Shimron, the Shimronite clan.
translator_notes: Yashuv (called Iob/Job in Genesis 46:13) and Shimron complete Issachar's four clans. The name Yashuv means 'he returns,' while Shimron may relate to shamar ('to guard/watch'). The variant Iob/Yashuv represents a more significant name change than most — possibly reflecting an alternative family tradition.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֛לֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹ֥ת יִשָּׂשכָ֖ר לִפְקֻדֵיהֶ֑ם אַרְבָּעָ֧ה וְשִׁשִּׁ֛ים אֶ֖לֶף וּשְׁלֹ֥שׁ מֵאֽוֹת׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: These are the families of Issachar according to those that were numbered of them, threescore and four thousand and three hundred.
verses.rendering: These are the Issachar clans. Their registered total was 64,300.
translator_notes: Issachar experienced significant growth: from 54,400 (Numbers 1:29) to 64,300 — a gain of 9,900, among the largest increases. The Hebrew arba'ah veshishim elef ('four and sixty thousand') uses the typical Hebrew number order (units before tens).

verses.text_hebrew: בְּנֵ֣י זְבוּלֻן֮ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם֒ לְסֶ֗רֶד מִשְׁפַּ֙חַת֙ הַסַּרְדִּ֔י לְאֵל֕וֹן מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הָאֵלֹנִ֑י לְיַ֨חְלְאֵ֔ל מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַיַּחְלְאֵלִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Of the sons of Zebulun after their families: of Sered, the family of the Sardites: of Elon, the family of the Elonites: of Jahleel, the family of the Jahleelites.
verses.rendering: The descendants of Zebulun by their clans: from Sered, the Seredite clan; from Elon, the Elonite clan; from Jahleel, the Jahleelite clan.
translator_notes: Zebulun has only three clans — the fewest among the twelve tribes (excluding Levi). Sered, Elon ('oak/terebinth'), and Yachle'el ('God waits/hopes') are consistent with Genesis 46:14. Despite having few clans, Zebulun maintained a respectable population.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֛לֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹ֥ת הַזְּבוּלֹנִ֖י לִפְקֻדֵיהֶ֑ם שִׁשִּׁ֥ים אֶ֖לֶף וַחֲמֵ֥שׁ מֵאֽוֹת׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: These are the families of the Zebulunites according to those that were numbered of them, threescore thousand and five hundred.
verses.rendering: These are the Zebulunite clans. Their registered total was 60,500.
translator_notes: Zebulun grew from 57,400 (Numbers 1:31) to 60,500 — an increase of 3,100. The tribe maintained strong numbers despite having only three clans, suggesting robust population growth within each clan. The KJV 'threescore' renders the Hebrew shishshim ('sixty').

verses.text_hebrew: בְּנֵ֥י יוֹסֵ֖ף לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָ֑ם מְנַשֶּׁ֖ה וְאֶפְרָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: The sons of Joseph after their families were Manasseh and Ephraim.
verses.rendering: The descendants of Joseph by their clans were Manasseh and Ephraim.
translator_notes: Joseph's two sons Menasheh and Efrayim each constitute a full tribe — Jacob's adoption of them (Genesis 48:5) effectively gave Joseph a double portion. Here Menasheh is listed first (birth order), though Ephraim received the primary blessing (Genesis 48:14-20). Together they replace the single 'tribe of Joseph.'

verses.text_hebrew: בְּנֵ֣י מְנַשֶּׁ֗ה לְמָכִיר֙ מִשְׁפַּ֣חַת הַמָּכִירִ֔י וּמָכִ֖יר הוֹלִ֣יד אֶת־גִּלְעָ֑ד לְגִלְעָ֕ד מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַגִּלְעָדִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Of the sons of Manasseh: of Machir, the family of the Machirites: and Machir begat Gilead: of Gilead come the family of the Gileadites.
verses.rendering: The descendants of Manasseh: from Machir, the Machirite clan — and Machir fathered Gilead; from Gilead, the Gileadite clan.
translator_notes: Manasseh's genealogy traces through a single line: Menasheh → Makhir → Gil'ad, then branches into six sub-clans (vv. 30-32). Makhir ('sold') was Manasseh's only son. Gil'ad ('rocky region') gives his name to the Transjordanian territory. The verb holid ('fathered, begot') marks the generational descent.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֚לֶּה בְּנֵ֣י גִלְעָ֔ד אִיעֶ֕זֶר מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הָאִֽיעֶזְרִ֑י לְחֵ֕לֶק מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַֽחֶלְקִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: These are the sons of Gilead: of Jeezer, the family of the Jeezerites: of Helek, the family of the Helekites:
verses.rendering: These are the sons of Gilead: from Iezer, the Iezerite clan; from Helek, the Helekite clan.
translator_notes: Gilead's sub-clans begin with I'ezer (abbreviated from Avi'ezer, 'my father is help') and Chelek ('portion'). These names recur in the land allocation of Joshua 17:2, where each Gileadite clan receives its territorial portion. The I'ezerite clan is associated with Gideon's family (Judges 6:11).

verses.text_hebrew: וְאַ֨שְׂרִיאֵ֔ל מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הָאַשְׂרִֽאֵלִ֑י וְשֶׁ֕כֶם מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַשִּׁכְמִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: And of Asriel, the family of the Asrielites: and of Shechem, the family of the Shechemites:
verses.rendering: From Asriel, the Asrielite clan; from Shechem, the Shechemite clan.
translator_notes: Asri'el ('God has blessed me/vow of God') and Shekhem ('shoulder, ridge') continue the Gileadite sub-clans. The name Shekhem connects this clan to the important central Canaanite city, though the relationship between the personal name and the place is debated.

verses.text_hebrew: וּשְׁמִידָ֕ע מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַשְּׁמִידָעִ֑י וְחֵ֕פֶר מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַֽחֶפְרִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: And of Shemida, the family of the Shemidaites: and of Hepher, the family of the Hepherites.
verses.rendering: From Shemida, the Shemidaite clan; from Hepher, the Hepherite clan.
translator_notes: Shemida ('name has known' or 'fame') and Chefer ('pit, well') complete the six Gileadite sub-clans of Manasseh. Chefer's clan is singled out in the next verse because of the Zelophehad inheritance case — one of the Torah's most significant precedents regarding women's property rights.

verses.text_hebrew: וּצְלׇפְחָ֣ד בֶּן־חֵ֗פֶר לֹא־הָ֥יוּ ל֛וֹ בָּנִ֖ים כִּ֣י אִם־בָּנ֑וֹת וְשֵׁם֙ בְּנ֣וֹת צְלׇפְחָ֔ד מַחְלָ֣ה וְנֹעָ֔ה חׇגְלָ֥ה מִלְכָּ֖ה וְתִרְצָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And Zelophehad the son of Hepher had no sons, but daughters: and the names of the daughters of Zelophehad were Mahlah, and Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.
verses.rendering: Zelophehad son of Hepher had no sons — only daughters. The names of Zelophehad's daughters were Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.
translator_notes: This genealogical note — lo-hayu lo vanim ki im-banot ('he had no sons, only daughters') — anticipates the landmark legal case in Numbers 27:1-11 where these five women petition for and receive inheritance rights. Their individual naming (Machlah, No'ah, Choglah, Milkah, Tirtzah) is unusual in census lists and underscores their legal significance. The name Tzelofchad may mean 'shadow of fear' or 'protection from dread.'

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֖לֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹ֣ת מְנַשֶּׁ֑ה וּפְקֻ֣דֵיהֶ֔ם שְׁנַ֧יִם וַחֲמִשִּׁ֛ים אֶ֖לֶף וּשְׁבַ֥ע מֵאֽוֹת׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: These are the families of Manasseh, and those that were numbered of them, fifty and two thousand and seven hundred.
verses.rendering: These are the Manassite clans. Their registered total was 52,700.
translator_notes: Manasseh experienced the largest growth of any tribe: from 32,200 (Numbers 1:35) to 52,700 — an increase of 20,500, or nearly 64%. This dramatic rise reversed the first census pattern where Ephraim was larger. Manasseh's expansion justified its claim to extensive Transjordanian territory.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֣לֶּה בְנֵי־אֶפְרַ֘יִם֮ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם֒ לְשׁוּתֶ֗לַח מִשְׁפַּ֙חַת֙ הַשֻּׁ֣תַלְחִ֔י לְבֶ֕כֶר מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַבַּכְרִ֑י לְתַ֕חַן מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַֽתַּחֲנִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: These are the sons of Ephraim after their families: of Shuthelah, the family of the Shuthalhites: of Becher, the family of the Bachrites: of Tahan, the family of the Tahanites.
verses.rendering: These are the descendants of Ephraim by their clans: from Shuthelah, the Shuthelahite clan; from Becher, the Becherite clan; from Tahan, the Tahanite clan.
translator_notes: Ephraim's three primary clans — Shutelach, Bekher ('firstborn'), and Tachan — are fewer than Manasseh's six sub-clans. The name Shutelach is of uncertain etymology; Tachan may mean 'encampment' or 'grace.' Ephraim's clan structure is simpler than Manasseh's multi-level genealogy.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאֵ֖לֶּה בְּנֵ֣י שׁוּתָ֑לַח לְעֵרָ֕ן מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הָעֵרָנִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: And these are the sons of Shuthelah: of Eran, the family of the Eranites.
verses.rendering: These are the sons of Shuthelah: from Eran, the Eranite clan.
translator_notes: Shuthelah produces one sub-clan through Eran ('watchful'), paralleling the Perez sub-clans in Judah (v. 21). This additional genealogical layer, present only for select clans, indicates which family lines achieved sufficient size and prominence to warrant independent recognition.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֣לֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹ֤ת בְּנֵי־אֶפְרַ֙יִם֙ לִפְקֻ֣דֵיהֶ֔ם שְׁנַ֧יִם וּשְׁלֹשִׁ֛ים אֶ֖לֶף וַחֲמֵ֣שׁ מֵא֑וֹת אֵ֥לֶּה בְנֵי־יוֹסֵ֖ף לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָֽם׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: These are the families of the sons of Ephraim according to those that were numbered of them, thirty and two thousand and five hundred. These are the sons of Joseph after their families.
verses.rendering: These are the Ephraimite clans. Their registered total was 32,500. These are the descendants of Joseph by their clans.
translator_notes: Ephraim declined from 40,500 (Numbers 1:33) to 32,500 — a loss of 8,000. Combined with Manasseh's surge, the 'house of Joseph' dynamic shifted: Manasseh (52,700) now far exceeds Ephraim (32,500), reversing Jacob's preferential blessing of Ephraim. The closing phrase elleh venei-Yosef lemishpechotam ('these are Joseph's descendants by their clans') brackets the two half-tribes as one unit.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּנֵ֣י בִנְיָמִן֮ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם֒ לְבֶ֗לַע מִשְׁפַּ֙חַת֙ הַבַּלְעִ֔י לְאַשְׁבֵּ֕ל מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הָֽאַשְׁבֵּלִ֑י לַאֲחִירָ֕ם מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הָאֲחִירָמִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: The sons of Benjamin after their families: of Bela, the family of the Belaites: of Ashbel, the family of the Ashbelites: of Ahiram, the family of the Ahiramites:
verses.rendering: The descendants of Benjamin by their clans: from Bela, the Belaite clan; from Ashbel, the Ashbelite clan; from Ahiram, the Ahiramite clan.
translator_notes: Benjamin lists five clans (with two sub-clans in v. 40), making seven total. Bela ('swallowing'), Ashbel, and Achiram (possibly the same as Ehi in Genesis 46:21) begin the list. Benjamin's clan names show the most variation between Genesis and Numbers of any tribe, likely reflecting clan mergers and name changes over the generations.

verses.text_hebrew: לִשְׁפוּפָ֕ם מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַשּׁוּפָמִ֑י לְחוּפָ֕ם מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַחוּפָמִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Of Shupham, the family of the Shuphamites: of Hupham, the family of the Huphamites.
verses.rendering: From Shephupham, the Shuphamite clan; from Hupham, the Huphamite clan.
translator_notes: Shefufam (gentilic HaShufami) and Chufam complete Benjamin's primary clans. These names correspond to Muppim and Huppim in Genesis 46:21, with significant phonetic variation. The gentilic forms normalize these variant spellings into standardized clan designations.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּהְי֥וּ בְנֵי־בֶ֖לַע אַ֣רְדְּ וְנַעֲמָ֑ן מִשְׁפַּ֙חַת֙ הָֽאַרְדִּ֔י לְנַֽעֲמָ֔ן מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַֽנַּעֲמִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: And the sons of Bela were Ard and Naaman: of Ard, the family of the Ardites: and of Naaman, the family of the Naamites.
verses.rendering: The sons of Bela were Ard and Naaman: from Ard, the Ardite clan; from Naaman, the Naamite clan.
translator_notes: Bela's sub-clans Ard and Na'aman ('pleasant, gracious') create a second genealogical tier within Benjamin, similar to Perez's sub-clans in Judah. Na'aman's name means 'pleasantness' — the same root as the later Aramean general's name (2 Kings 5). These seven total Benjaminite clans sustained a tribe that would produce Israel's first king.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֥לֶּה בְנֵי־בִנְיָמִ֖ן לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָ֑ם וּפְקֻ֣דֵיהֶ֔ם חֲמִשָּׁ֧ה וְאַרְבָּעִ֛ים אֶ֖לֶף וְשֵׁ֥שׁ מֵאֽוֹת׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: These are the sons of Benjamin after their families: and they that were numbered of them were forty and five thousand and six hundred.
verses.rendering: These are the Benjaminite clans. Their registered total was 45,600.
translator_notes: Benjamin grew from 35,400 (Numbers 1:37) to 45,600 — an increase of 10,200, the second-largest gain after Manasseh. This growth is especially notable given Benjamin's small initial size. The tribe's expansion foreshadowed its later disproportionate influence in Israel's history, producing King Saul and the apostle Paul.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֤לֶּה בְנֵי־דָן֙ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָ֔ם לְשׁוּחָ֕ם מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַשּׁוּחָמִ֑י אֵ֛לֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹ֥ת דָּ֖ן לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: These are the sons of Dan after their families: of Shuham, the family of the Shuhamites. These are the families of Dan after their families.
verses.rendering: The descendants of Dan by their clans: from Shuham, the Shuhamite clan. These are the clans of Dan by their families.
translator_notes: Dan has only a single clan — Shucham (called Hushim in Genesis 46:23). Despite this remarkably simple genealogical structure, Dan was one of the largest tribes (v. 43). The repetitive phrasing elleh mishpechot Dan lemishpechotam ('these are Dan's clans by their families') fills the space where other tribes list multiple clans.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל־מִשְׁפְּחֹ֥ת הַשּׁוּחָמִ֖י לִפְקֻדֵיהֶ֑ם אַרְבָּעָ֧ה וְשִׁשִּׁ֛ים אֶ֖לֶף וְאַרְבַּ֥ע מֵאֽוֹת׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: All the families of the Shuhamites, according to those that were numbered of them, were threescore and four thousand and four hundred.
verses.rendering: All the Shuhamite clans totaled 64,400 in their registration.
translator_notes: Dan grew slightly from 62,700 (Numbers 1:39) to 64,400 — an increase of 1,700. Despite having only one clan, Dan was the second-largest tribe (after Judah's 76,500). The phrase kol-mishpechot haShuchami ('all the clans of the Shuhamites') uses the plural even though only one clan name is given, possibly indicating internal sub-divisions not formally listed.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּנֵ֣י אָשֵׁר֮ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם֒ לְיִמְנָ֗ה מִשְׁפַּ֙חַת֙ הַיִּמְנָ֔ה לְיִשְׁוִ֕י מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַיִּשְׁוִ֑י לִבְרִיעָ֕ה מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַבְּרִיעִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Of the children of Asher after their families: of Jimna, the family of the Jimnites: of Jesui, the family of the Jesuites: of Beriah, the family of the Beriites.
verses.rendering: The descendants of Asher by their clans: from Imnah, the Imnite clan; from Ishvi, the Ishvite clan; from Beriah, the Beriite clan.
translator_notes: Asher's three primary clans — Yimnah ('prosperity, right hand'), Yishvi ('equal, level'), and Beri'ah ('in evil/misfortune' or 'excellence') — are supplemented by Beriah's sub-clans in verse 45. A fourth son, Ishuah, listed in Genesis 46:17, is absent here — his line may have died out or been absorbed into another clan.

verses.text_hebrew: לִבְנֵ֣י בְרִיעָ֔ה לְחֶ֕בֶר מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַֽחֶבְרִ֑י לְמַ֨לְכִּיאֵ֔ל מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַמַּלְכִּיאֵלִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Of the sons of Beriah: of Heber, the family of the Heberites: of Malchiel, the family of the Malchielites.
verses.rendering: Among the sons of Beriah: from Heber, the Heberite clan; from Malchiel, the Malchielite clan.
translator_notes: Beriah's sub-clans Chever ('companion, associate') and Malki'el ('my king is God') add depth to Asher's genealogy. Malki'el is a theophoric name expressing devotion to God as king — one of several such names in the census. These five total Asherite clans (three primary plus two sub-clans) sustained a mid-sized tribe.

verses.text_hebrew: וְשֵׁ֥ם בַּת־אָשֵׁ֖ר שָֽׂרַח׃
verses.text_kjv: And the name of the daughter of Asher was Sarah.
verses.rendering: The name of Asher's daughter was Serah.
translator_notes: This brief note — veshem bat-Asher Serach — is remarkable: Serah is one of very few women named in a census list. She also appears in Genesis 46:17, suggesting she held a uniquely honored position in tribal memory. Rabbinic tradition attributes extraordinary longevity and wisdom to her. Her presence in a military census list is striking and unexplained in the text.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֛לֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹ֥ת בְּנֵי־אָשֵׁ֖ר לִפְקֻדֵיהֶ֑ם שְׁלֹשָׁ֧ה וַחֲמִשִּׁ֛ים אֶ֖לֶף וְאַרְבַּ֥ע מֵאֽוֹת׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: These are the families of the sons of Asher according to those that were numbered of them; who were fifty and three thousand and four hundred.
verses.rendering: These are the Asherite clans. Their registered total was 53,400.
translator_notes: Asher grew from 41,500 (Numbers 1:41) to 53,400 — a substantial increase of 11,900. This makes Asher one of the fastest-growing tribes, consistent with Moses' later blessing that Asher would be 'most blessed of sons' and would 'dip his foot in oil' (Deuteronomy 33:24).

verses.text_hebrew: בְּנֵ֤י נַפְתָּלִי֙ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָ֔ם לְיַ֨חְצְאֵ֔ל מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַיַּחְצְאֵלִ֑י לְגוּנִ֕י מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַגּוּנִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Of the sons of Naphtali after their families: of Jahzeel, the family of the Jahzeelites: of Guni, the family of the Gunites:
verses.rendering: The descendants of Naphtali by their clans: from Jahzeel, the Jahzeelite clan; from Guni, the Gunite clan.
translator_notes: Naphtali's four clans begin with Yachtze'el ('God apportions') and Guni ('my protector' or 'painted/colored'). These names are consistent with Genesis 46:24. Naphtali's territory would later occupy the fertile Galilee region — the area where much of Jesus' ministry would take place centuries later.

verses.text_hebrew: לְיֵ֕צֶר מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַיִּצְרִ֑י לְשִׁלֵּ֕ם מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַשִּׁלֵּמִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Of Jezer, the family of the Jezerites: of Shillem, the family of the Shillemites.
verses.rendering: From Jezer, the Jezerite clan; from Shillem, the Shillemite clan.
translator_notes: Yetzer ('form, inclination') and Shillem ('recompense, repayment') complete Naphtali's four clans. The name Shillem shares the root sh-l-m with shalom and shelamim, connecting to concepts of completion and wholeness. Naphtali's compact four-clan structure mirrors Issachar's.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֛לֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹ֥ת נַפְתָּלִ֖י לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָ֑ם וּפְקֻ֣דֵיהֶ֔ם חֲמִשָּׁ֧ה וְאַרְבָּעִ֛ים אֶ֖לֶף וְאַרְבַּ֥ע מֵאֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: These are the families of Naphtali according to their families: and they that were numbered of them were forty and five thousand and four hundred.
verses.rendering: These are the Naphtali clans by their families. Their registered total was 45,400.
translator_notes: Naphtali decreased from 53,400 (Numbers 1:43) to 45,400 — a loss of 8,000. This significant decline parallels Ephraim's reduction. The phrase chamishshah ve'arba'im elef ('forty-five thousand') concludes the tribal census proper before the grand total in verse 51.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֗לֶּה פְּקוּדֵי֙ בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל שֵׁשׁ־מֵא֥וֹת אֶ֖לֶף וָאָ֑לֶף שְׁבַ֥ע מֵא֖וֹת וּשְׁלֹשִֽׁים׃ {פ}
verses.text_kjv: These were the numbered of the children of Israel, six hundred thousand and a thousand seven hundred and thirty.
verses.rendering: These were the total registered Israelites: 601,730.
translator_notes: The grand total of 601,730 is remarkably close to the first census total of 603,550 (Numbers 1:46) — a net decrease of only 1,820 despite the massive losses to plague, war, and divine judgment. The Hebrew shesh-me'ot elef va'elef sheva me'ot usheloshim ('six hundred thousand and a thousand, seven hundred and thirty') expresses the figure in a distinctive compound form. The near-stability of the overall population amid dramatic individual tribal shifts demonstrates divine faithfulness to the patriarchal promise of numerous descendants.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְדַבֵּ֥ר יְהֹוָ֖ה אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֥ה לֵּאמֹֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.rendering: The LORD spoke to Moses:
translator_notes: The divine speech formula transitions from the census enumeration to its practical purpose — land allocation. The census was not merely demographic record-keeping but the basis for dividing the inheritance of the Promised Land among the tribes.

verses.text_hebrew: לָאֵ֗לֶּה תֵּחָלֵ֥ק הָאָ֛רֶץ בְּנַחֲלָ֖ה בְּמִסְפַּ֥ר שֵׁמֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: Unto these the land shall be divided for an inheritance according to the number of names.
verses.rendering: To these the land is to be apportioned as an inheritance based on the number of registered names.
translator_notes: The verb techalek ('shall be divided/apportioned') from chalaq ('to divide, distribute') establishes the census-to-land connection. The phrase bemispar shemot ('by the number of names') means each person counted represents a claim on the land. The nachalah ('inheritance') is not mere property but a covenantal grant — permanent tribal territory within the Promised Land.

key_terms.note: Denotes not just property but covenantal land-grant; inalienable tribal territory tied to God's promise

verses.text_hebrew: לָרַ֗ב תַּרְבֶּה֙ נַחֲלָת֔וֹ וְלַמְעַ֕ט תַּמְעִ֖יט נַחֲלָת֑וֹ אִ֚ישׁ לְפִ֣י פְקֻדָ֔יו יֻתַּ֖ן נַחֲלָתֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: To many thou shalt give the more inheritance, and to few thou shalt give the less inheritance: to every one shall his inheritance be given according to those that were numbered of him.
verses.rendering: To the larger group, increase its inheritance; to the smaller group, reduce its inheritance. Each tribe receives its inheritance proportional to its registered count.
translator_notes: The principle is proportional allocation: larav tarbeh nachalato ('for the many, increase its inheritance') and lam'at tam'it nachalato ('for the few, reduce its inheritance'). The verbs tarbeh and tam'it ('increase' and 'decrease') create a balanced pair. This ensures demographic fairness — larger tribes receive more land to support their population, smaller tribes less.

verses.text_hebrew: אַךְ־בְּגוֹרָ֕ל יֵחָלֵ֖ק אֶת־הָאָ֑רֶץ לִשְׁמ֥וֹת מַטּוֹת־אֲבֹתָ֖ם יִנְחָֽלוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Notwithstanding the land shall be divided by lot: according to the names of the tribes of their fathers they shall inherit.
verses.rendering: However, the land is to be distributed by lot. They will receive their inheritance according to the names of their ancestral tribes.
translator_notes: The akh ('however, only') introduces a qualification: despite the proportional principle, the actual location of each tribe's allotment is determined begoral ('by lot'). The goral ('lot') introduces a divine element — God controls the outcome (Proverbs 16:33). This creates a dual system: lot determines where; population size determines how much. The phrase lishemot mattot-avotam ('according to the names of their ancestral tribes') maintains tribal cohesion.

key_terms.semantic_range: lot, portion assigned by lot, fate, destiny
key_terms.note: A decision-making device believed to reveal divine will; possibly stones or sticks drawn from a container

verses.text_hebrew: עַל־פִּי֙ הַגּוֹרָ֔ל תֵּחָלֵ֖ק נַחֲלָת֑וֹ בֵּ֥ין רַ֖ב לִמְעָֽט׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: According to the lot shall the possession thereof be divided between many and few.
verses.rendering: By the decision of the lot, each inheritance is to be apportioned, whether the tribe is large or small.
translator_notes: The phrase al-pi haGoral ('according to the mouth/decision of the lot') personifies the lot as having a 'mouth' — it speaks God's will. The final bein rav lim'at ('between many and few') reaffirms that both large and small tribes participate in the same lot-based system. This concludes the land-allocation legislation triggered by the census.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאֵ֨לֶּה פְקוּדֵ֣י הַלֵּוִי֮ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם֒ לְגֵרְשׁ֗וֹן מִשְׁפַּ֙חַת֙ הַגֵּ֣רְשֻׁנִּ֔י לִקְהָ֕ת מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַקְּהָתִ֑י לִמְרָרִ֕י מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַמְּרָרִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: And these are they that were numbered of the Levites after their families: of Gershon, the family of the Gershonites: of Kohath, the family of the Kohathites: of Merari, the family of the Merarites.
verses.rendering: These are the registered Levites by their clans: from Gershon, the Gershonite clan; from Kohath, the Kohathite clan; from Merari, the Merarite clan.
translator_notes: The Levites receive a separate census because they receive no territorial inheritance (v. 62). The three primary Levitical divisions — Gershon, Qehat, and Merari — trace to Levi's three sons (Genesis 46:11). Each had distinct tabernacle responsibilities: Gershonites handled the tent fabrics, Kohathites the sacred furniture, and Merarites the structural framework.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֣לֶּה ׀ מִשְׁפְּחֹ֣ת לֵוִ֗י מִשְׁפַּ֨חַת הַלִּבְנִ֜י מִשְׁפַּ֤חַת הַֽחֶבְרֹנִי֙ מִשְׁפַּ֤חַת הַמַּחְלִי֙ מִשְׁפַּ֣חַת הַמּוּשִׁ֔י מִשְׁפַּ֖חַת הַקׇּרְחִ֑י וּקְהָ֖ת הוֹלִ֥ד אֶת־עַמְרָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: These are the families of the Levites: the family of the Libnites, the family of the Hebronites, the family of the Mahlites, the family of the Mushites, the family of the Korathites. And Kohath begat Amram.
verses.rendering: These are the Levitical clans: the Libnite clan, the Hebronite clan, the Mahlite clan, the Mushite clan, and the Korahite clan. Kohath fathered Amram.
translator_notes: Five Levitical sub-clans are listed without being assigned to their parent divisions — HaLibni (from Gershon), HaChevroni and HaQorchi (from Kohath), HaMachli and HaMushi (from Merari). The genealogical note uQehat holid et-Amram ('Kohath fathered Amram') transitions to the priestly lineage. This bridges from tribal census to priestly succession.

verses.text_hebrew: וְשֵׁ֣ם ׀ אֵ֣שֶׁת עַמְרָ֗ם יוֹכֶ֙בֶד֙ בַּת־לֵוִ֔י אֲשֶׁ֨ר יָלְדָ֥ה אֹתָ֛הּ לְלֵוִ֖י בְּמִצְרָ֑יִם וַתֵּ֣לֶד לְעַמְרָ֗ם אֶֽת־אַהֲרֹן֙ וְאֶת־מֹשֶׁ֔ה וְאֵ֖ת מִרְיָ֥ם אֲחֹתָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And the name of Amram's wife was Jochebed, the daughter of Levi, whom her mother bare to Levi in Egypt: and she bare unto Amram Aaron and Moses, and Miriam their sister.
verses.rendering: The name of Amram's wife was Jochebed, a daughter of Levi who was born to Levi in Egypt. She bore to Amram: Aaron, Moses, and their sister Miriam.
translator_notes: Yokheved ('YHVH is glory') is remarkable as the first person in the Torah with a name containing the divine name element Yo-. She is identified as bat-Levi ('a daughter of Levi'), meaning a descendant of the tribe. The phrase asher yaldah otah leLevi beMitzrayim ('who was born to Levi in Egypt') locates her birth in Egypt. She bore Israel's three most important leaders: Aharon (priest), Mosheh (prophet/lawgiver), and Miryam (prophetess).

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּוָּלֵ֣ד לְאַהֲרֹ֔ן אֶת־נָדָ֖ב וְאֶת־אֲבִיה֑וּא אֶת־אֶלְעָזָ֖ר וְאֶת־אִיתָמָֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: And unto Aaron was born Nadab, and Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.
verses.rendering: To Aaron were born Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.
translator_notes: Aaron's four sons are listed in birth order: Nadav ('generous'), Avihu ('he is my father'), El'azar ('God has helped'), and Itamar ('palm coast'). The passive vayyivvaled ('were born') emphasizes the divine gift of progeny. The first two will be noted as dead in the next verse, leaving the priestly line to continue through the latter two.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיָּ֥מׇת נָדָ֖ב וַאֲבִיה֑וּא בְּהַקְרִיבָ֥ם אֵשׁ־זָרָ֖ה לִפְנֵ֥י יְהֹוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And Nadab and Abihu died, when they offered strange fire before the LORD.
verses.rendering: Nadab and Abihu died when they presented unauthorized fire before the LORD.
translator_notes: The phrase behaqqrivam esh-zarah ('when they brought near foreign/strange fire') refers to the catastrophic incident of Leviticus 10:1-2. The esh zarah ('unauthorized/alien fire') violated the prescribed method of offering. Their death lifnei YHVH ('before the LORD') — in the very sanctuary where God's presence dwelled — made it a paradigmatic warning about sacred boundaries.

key_terms.semantic_range: strange fire, foreign fire, alien fire, unauthorized incense
key_terms.note: Fire or incense not prescribed by God's explicit command; the nature of the violation is debated

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּהְי֣וּ פְקֻדֵיהֶ֗ם שְׁלֹשָׁ֤ה וְעֶשְׂרִים֙ אֶ֔לֶף כׇּל־זָכָ֖ר מִבֶּן־חֹ֣דֶשׁ וָמָ֑עְלָה כִּ֣י ׀ לֹ֣א הׇתְפָּקְד֗וּ בְּתוֹךְ֙ בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל כִּ֠י לֹא־נִתַּ֤ן לָהֶם֙ נַחֲלָ֔ה בְּת֖וֹךְ בְּנֵ֥י יִשְׂרָאֵֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: And those that were numbered of them were twenty and three thousand, all males from a month old and upward: for they were not numbered among the children of Israel, because there was no inheritance given them among the children of Israel.
verses.rendering: Their registered total was 23,000 — every male from one month old and above. They were not counted among the other Israelites because no territorial inheritance was given to them among the Israelites.
translator_notes: The Levites totaled 23,000, up from 22,000 at the first census (Numbers 3:39) — a modest gain of 1,000. Two key differences from the tribal census: (1) they were counted mibben-chodesh vama'lah ('from one month old and above') rather than twenty years, and (2) they received no nachalah ('inheritance') because God Himself was their inheritance (Numbers 18:20). The phrase lo hotpaqdu betokh benei-Yisra'el ('they were not counted among the Israelites') explains their separate enumeration.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֚לֶּה פְּקוּדֵ֣י מֹשֶׁ֔ה וְאֶלְעָזָ֖ר הַכֹּהֵ֑ן אֲשֶׁ֨ר פָּֽקְד֜וּ אֶת־בְּנֵ֤י יִשְׂרָאֵל֙ בְּעַֽרְבֹ֣ת מוֹאָ֔ב עַ֖ל יַרְדֵּ֥ן יְרֵחֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: These are they that were numbered by Moses and Eleazar the priest, who numbered the children of Israel in the plains of Moab by Jordan near Jericho.
verses.rendering: These are those registered by Moses and Eleazar the priest when they counted the Israelites in the plains of Moab beside the Jordan, across from Jericho.
translator_notes: The concluding formula mirrors the opening (v. 3), creating an inclusio — paqdu et-benei Yisra'el be'arvot Mo'av ('they counted the Israelites in the plains of Moab'). Moses and Eleazar jointly conducted this census, as Aaron had jointly conducted the first with Moses (Numbers 1:3). The geographic marker al Yarden Yerecho ('by the Jordan at Jericho') emphasizes proximity to the Promised Land.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבְאֵ֙לֶּה֙ לֹא־הָ֣יָה אִ֔ישׁ מִפְּקוּדֵ֣י מֹשֶׁ֔ה וְאַהֲרֹ֖ן הַכֹּהֵ֑ן אֲשֶׁ֥ר פָּקְד֛וּ אֶת־בְּנֵ֥י יִשְׂרָאֵ֖ל בְּמִדְבַּ֥ר סִינָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: But among these there was not a man of them whom Moses and Aaron the priest numbered, when they numbered the children of Israel in the wilderness of Sinai.
verses.rendering: Among these there was not a single man from those registered by Moses and Aaron the priest when they counted the Israelites in the wilderness of Sinai.
translator_notes: The devastating verdict: uve'elleh lo-hayah ish ('among these there was not a man') from the first census. The juxtaposition of the two census locations — bemidbar Sinai ('in the wilderness of Sinai') versus be'arvot Mo'av (v. 63) — spans the entire wilderness period. An entire generation vanished between these two counts, fulfilling God's judgment in Numbers 14:29.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־אָמַ֤ר יְהֹוָה֙ לָהֶ֔ם מ֥וֹת יָמֻ֖תוּ בַּמִּדְבָּ֑ר וְלֹא־נוֹתַ֤ר מֵהֶם֙ אִ֔ישׁ כִּ֚י אִם־כָּלֵ֣ב בֶּן־יְפֻנֶּ֔ה וִיהוֹשֻׁ֖עַ בִּן־נֽוּן׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: For the LORD had said of them, They shall surely die in the wilderness. And there was not left a man of them, save Caleb the son of Jephunneh, and Joshua the son of Nun.
verses.rendering: For the LORD had declared about them: They will certainly die in the wilderness. Not one of them survived except Caleb son of Jephunneh and Joshua son of Nun.
translator_notes: The chapter closes with the two exceptions: Kalev ben-Yefunneh and Yehoshua bin-Nun — the only faithful spies (Numbers 14:6-9). The infinitive absolute mot yamuthu ('dying they shall die') intensified the death decree. The verb notar ('remained, survived') emphasizes that literally no one else from the Sinai census lived to reach this point. These two men bridge the generations, embodying the reward of covenant faithfulness amid universal judgment.
